items.title: 69% concentration — the narrowest rally in 150 years of S&P data
items.idea: Since March 30, the S&P has rallied +16%. But 10 stocks drove 69% of that return: Alphabet alone delivered 15% of the index's entire gain, Nvidia 10%, Amazon 8%, Broadcom 7%, Intel 6%, Micron 6%. The equal-weight S&P is up only +5.8% YTD vs. cap-weight +13.4% — a 750bp spread, widest since 2007. The Russell 2000 is DOWN -2.4% YTD while headline indices hit ATH. Cap-to-equal 60-day correlation just hit +0.41, lowest since March 2000. Prior concentration peaks: Nifty Fifty 1972 at 62% then S&P fell -37% cumulative; 1999 at 52% then S&P fell -49%; 2007 at 41% then S&P fell -38%. The author says there is not a single counterexample in 35 years of data.
items.angle: The number I can't shake: 69% concentration exceeds the 1972 Nifty Fifty peak (62%) which preceded a decade of real-return destruction. This isn't a warning — it's a historical signature. Trade: RSP vs. SPY spread, equal-weight expressions, and anything that benefits from mean reversion in breadth.
items.tweetA: The S&P is up +16% since March 30. 69% of that came from 10 stocks. Alphabet alone = 15% of the ENTIRE index return. In 1972 concentration peaked at 62% — then the S&P lost 37% over two years. We're at 69%. No counterexample exists in 35 years of data. Not one.
items.tweetB: $RSP (equal-weight S&P) up 5.8% YTD. $SPX up 13.4%. $IWM DOWN 2.4% on the year. This isn't a stock market — it's a basket of 10 AI-adjacent names with 490 passengers. The cap-to-equal 60-day correlation just hit its lowest since March 2000. That's the tell.

charts.desc: 8-chapter letter with charts: cap-to-equal correlation, top-10 contribution vs. historical analogs, YTD performance spreads. CHART WORTH SAVING: Nifty Fifty / 1999 / 2007 top-10 contribution overlay showing current reading at 69% vs. all prior peaks.

items.title: Global M2 at +11.4% YoY — Argentina the only adult, and what 100% of prior instances predicted
items.idea: Every major economy is expanding money supply: Colombia +30%, Mexico +27%, Uruguay +26%, Brazil +21%, Australia +20%, China +14%, Russia +14%, Eurozone +11%, US +7%. Only Japan (-4%) and Argentina (-11%) are different. Argentina's peso fell -97.2% in purchasing power since 2020 (40 to 1,400 ARS/USD). Current global M2 weighted at +11.4% YoY — inside the historical band that has produced gold >+15% annualized over 36 months in 100% of prior instances since 1970. Rolling 5-year correlation: M2 vs. gold +0.71, vs. DXY -0.62, vs. EM FX +0.54.
items.angle: The 100% hit rate claim is extraordinary and the correlation structure is strong. This is the liquidity case for gold and commodities in precise data form. Combined with the concentration story, the macro setup is Nifty Fifty meets 1970s — both at once.
items.tweetA: Colombia +30% M2. Mexico +27%. EU +11%. US +7%. Every major economy printing. Argentina at -11% is the only one tightening — and the peso already collapsed 97% over 5 years. Current global M2 at +11.4% YoY: inside the band that has produced gold >+15% annualized over 36 months in 100% of prior instances since 1970. 100%.
items.tweetB: Argentina's peso went from 40 to 1,400 per dollar since 2020. -97.2% purchasing power. Now Milei's contraction is being called 'the only adult in the room.' That's what monetary discipline looks like when it arrives 20 years too late. The rest of the world is on the same clock. We just have more runway left.

charts.desc: Global M2 heatmap chart showing every major economy's USD-denominated M2 growth. Charts inside the full letter.

items.title: Shiller P/E at 37.9 (2nd-highest in 150 years) + Fed bought $237B Treasuries since December
items.idea: Shiller P/E now at 37.9 — the 2nd-highest reading in 150 years of recorded data, topped only by the 2000 dot-com peak. Meanwhile the Fed has quietly bought $237B in Treasuries since December at a pace not seen since 2008. The Bloomberg Commodity Index has punched through to a 13-year high. The author frames this as three things that shouldn't coexist: extreme valuation, stealth QE, and commodity breakout. The only historical analog for all three together costs the average 60/40 portfolio an entire decade of real returns.
items.angle: The $237B Fed Treasury buying figure is the buried lede. If confirmed, this is stealth QE happening while the Fed publicly holds rates — exactly the kind of policy contradiction that eventually resolves violently. Cross-check against SOMA data. BCOM at 13-year high is the real-asset confirmation.
items.tweetA: Shiller P/E at 37.9. Only exceeded by the 2000 dot-com peak in 150 years of data. The Fed has quietly bought $237B in Treasuries since December (pace not seen since 2008). Bloomberg Commodities at a 13-year high. Three things that shouldn't coexist. Yet here we are.
items.tweetB: Bloomberg Commodity Index at a 13-year high. Nobody's talking about this because equities are the drug. But commodities screaming at 13-year highs while equities re-rate to the 2nd-most-expensive level in 150 years is either the best of all worlds — or the perfect setup for a violent rotation into real assets.

charts.desc: Shiller CAPE 150-year chart + BCOM 13-year chart showing the breakout to highs. CHART WORTH SAVING: BCOM breakout.

items.title: Project Freedom failed: Saudi Arabia pulled overflight rights; 70% of Iran's missiles still intact
items.idea: The US escort convoy operation (Project Freedom) failed: shipping companies didn't find the protection credible, Saudi Arabia refused basing and overflight rights, only two US-flagged Maersk ships participated. Per a CIA analysis leaked to the Hill: 70% of Iran's pre-war missile force is still intact or reconstituted. The Strait Q-route is too narrow for two-way traffic; mine clearance takes weeks post-ceasefire. Lt Gen Jack Shanahan (founding JAIC director): 'This is bereft of strategic thought.'
items.angle: The 70% figure is the most important number from this call. The entire narrative of the Iran campaign's success is built on degrading Iranian capability. If 70% is still standing, the Strait is not secured and shipping won't return at scale.
items.tweetA: Buried in the ChinaTalk WarTalk with Lt Gen Jack Shanahan: 70% of Iran's pre-war missile force is still intact. After all that. Project Freedom — the US convoy escort — failed because shipping companies didn't trust the protection and Saudi Arabia pulled overflight rights. 'Bereft of strategic thought.' — founding JAIC director.
items.tweetB: 'There's no such thing as an air campaign. There's a joint campaign of which there is an air component.' — Retired USAF Lt Gen Jack Shanahan. The US is fighting a conventional war. Iran is fighting an economic one. 900 large ships still stranded. Strait still effectively closed. Who's winning?

charts.desc: Podcast transcript. No charts.

items.title: Iran hits AWS data centers — AI infrastructure is now a military target
items.idea: Iran struck AWS data centers in the region, explicitly declaring digital/AI infrastructure as legitimate military targets. Shanahan: 'That reinforces why in the world are we going to build $500B Stargate infrastructure in the Middle East?' Eric Robinson: 'They put data centers into the concept of critical infrastructure very aggressively. This was true innovation.' US-China shipbuilding ratio: 232:1 annually (China leads), lowering the nuclear response threshold in any Western Pacific conflict analogously to how Soviet conventional superiority did in the Cold War.
items.angle: Every hyperscaler with Middle East data center exposure just had its threat model updated. The implication for AI buildout geography — Stargate, AWS MENA, Oracle UAE — is that physical presence in conflict zones carries tail risk not priced into capex plans.
items.tweetA: Iran hitting AWS data centers wasn't collateral damage. It was doctrine. They've declared AI/data infrastructure as legitimate military targets. Every hyperscaler building in the Middle East now has a threat model they didn't have before. Stargate's geography just became a strategic question.
items.tweetB: US vs. China shipbuilding: 232 to 1 annually. China leads. That ratio lowers the nuclear threshold in a Western Pacific conflict the same way Soviet conventional superiority did in the Cold War. The Iran war isn't a sideshow — it's the preview reel for a different theater.

charts.desc: No charts. Full transcript worth reading.

items.title: 30 MQ-9s shot down, 4 F-15Es lost — EW hollowed out for 15 years, Pacific implications
items.idea: 30 MQ-9s shot down in the Iran conflict ($30M each = ~$900M). 4 F-15Es lost (out of 218 total), 3 by Kuwaiti fratricide. The crown jewels — electromagnetic signatures, visual, acoustic profiles — recovered by Iran and shared with Russia/China. Shanahan: the US did not invest in electronic warfare for 15 years during CT/COIN. 'If we do not go all in on electromagnetic spectrum operations, we're in serious trouble in a fight in the Pacific.'
items.angle: The EW deficit is the single most important structural lesson from this conflict for defense tech investors. Clear signal for where procurement goes next: cheap mass-produced drones, EW platforms, counter-drone systems.
items.tweetA: 30 MQ-9s shot down in the Iran conflict. ~$900M gone. Each wreck gets recovered by Iranian intel and rebuilt. Electromagnetic signatures, acoustic profiles, classified capabilities — all now in adversary hands. 15 years of not investing in EW during COIN is presenting its bill. In the Pacific, this is the bill you can't pay.
items.tweetB: 'There is no such thing as an air campaign.' Lt Gen Shanahan drilled this in. The US fought conventionally. Iran fought asymmetrically — ballistic missiles, Shaheds, economic pressure. The lesson for defense tech: it's not about more exquisite platforms. It's about cheap, mass, redundant, and EW-hardened.

charts.desc: No charts.

items.title: 'Memorial Day Math' — 2 weeks to gas-price political test; 'bright glow' nuclear signaling
items.idea: The analysts frame Memorial Day weekend (2 weeks) as the first major political test: if gas is $5-6/gallon, the election question shifts from national security to economic. The administration bypassed Congress, losing the constitutional framework for civic virtue around military expenditure. Trump referenced a 'bright glow' coming from Iran if US ships are successfully attacked — which Shanahan reads as possible nuclear signaling. 'Without clarity in strategic end state, this is not going to end well.'
items.angle: Memorial Day is the political inflection point. It converts gas price from an economic statistic into a lived experience for 300M Americans on a holiday weekend. If the ceasefire doesn't hold, everything reprices.
items.tweetA: Memorial Day is in 2 weeks. $5-6/gallon gas = the political question shifts from 'are we winning the war?' to 'why is my life more expensive?' The administration never asked Congress to vote for this war — burning the rally-to-flag moment. Shanahan: 'It's open-ended. When it's open-ended, people only hear gas prices.'
items.tweetB: The president referenced a 'bright glow' coming from Iran if US ships are successfully attacked. That's nuclear signaling language. We're one warship shoot-down from an escalation ladder that nobody has mapped. 'This operation is bereft of strategic thought.' — Lt Gen Jack Shanahan, founding director of the Joint AI Center.

items.title: Brent -8% on peace memo, then rebounded — 13M bpd disrupted, JPMorgan: buffers now eroding
items.idea: Brent fell nearly 8% to $101.27 on Wednesday on peace talk news. WTI fell to $95.08. By Friday, Iran had fired missiles at the UAE and the US struck two Iranian tankers, lifting Brent 1% back. Both benchmarks ended -6%+ for the week. JPMorgan: supply buffers 'are now eroding and demand destruction will intensify.' 13M bpd remains disrupted. Diesel above $5/gallon. Fertilizer trade through Hormuz still constrained. December crude futures already trade below $70 vs. spot at ~$95 — a $25 contango pricing peace that hasn't arrived.
items.angle: The $25 contango between spot and December is the trade. If the ceasefire holds, December convergence upward is not priced. If it fails, spot collapses toward December. Either way the basis is mispriced.
items.tweetA: Brent fell 8% in one day on peace memo headlines. Then Iran fired missiles at the UAE. Then the US struck Iranian tankers. Oil is back to $101. 13M bpd disrupted. JPMorgan says supply buffers 'are now eroding.' December crude futures already price below $70. The market is pricing a resolution that hasn't happened.
items.tweetB: $25 contango between spot Brent (~$101) and December crude (sub-$70). Either this war resolves and spot crashes 30%, or it drags on and December is deeply mispriced. Fertilizer trade through Hormuz still constrained. 23,000 seafarers stranded. This isn't an oil trade. It's a food/fertilizer trade.

charts.desc: Weekly price movement table. Charts inside article.

items.title: NFP April: establishment +115k beats, but household survey -226k — 4th consecutive monthly decline
items.idea: Establishment survey: April NFP +115k, above every Bloomberg estimate (consensus: +60k). But household survey: employed persons fell -226k (4th consecutive monthly decline), labor force shrank -92k, participation at 61.8% (lowest since Oct 2021). IT sector: -13k (16th straight monthly decline, -342k or -11% from Nov 2022 peak). Federal government: -9k, now at lowest since April 1966 (-348k from Oct 2024 peak, -11.5%). U-6 rose to 8.2%. Part-time for economic reasons: +445k to 4.9M. Full-time jobs: -424k; part-time: +123k.
items.angle: The divergence between establishment and household surveys is at its most extreme. Every soft indicator is deteriorating simultaneously with a headline beat. The aggregate weekly payrolls metric rebounded to +0.58% m/m but the composition is weaker than it looks.
items.tweetA: April NFP: +115k headline, beat every estimate. Same month. Household survey: -226k employed persons (4th consecutive monthly decline). Labor force -92k. Part-time for economic reasons +445k. Full-time jobs -424k. Two surveys measuring the same economy, telling opposite stories. Which one do you trust?
items.tweetB: IT sector jobs: -13k in April. The 16th consecutive monthly decline. Down 342k or -11% from its November 2022 peak — lowest since March 2021. Federal government at its lowest employment level since April 1966. The 'tech recovery' is Nvidia up 40%. The labor reality is 16 straight months of IT cuts.

charts.desc: 8 charts: Federal government employment all-time, Labor force participation, Part-time for economic reasons, Full/part-time breakdown, U-6. Charts inside full post.

items.title: $5T HF AUM masks 22.5pp return spread — independent fund launches are functionally dead
items.idea: The hedge fund industry hit $5T AUM for the first time, but April showed extreme bifurcation (S&P +10%, second-best month since 2009): top decile +18.4%, bottom decile -4.1%, a 22.5pp spread. Independent launches are structurally broken: Kurt Baker (20yr Morgan Stanley, head of Millennium Asia) tried to raise $3B, got to 50%, never launched. Bobby Jain (7yr Millennium co-CIO) launched Jain Global only with $750M Millennium seed capital. Need minimum $500M AUM to keep costs at 3-5%. Jane Street: $2.7M average comp per employee, 700 people, $1.9B total comp.
items.angle: The independent launch death is the structural change that matters for talent flow. It concentrates the best PMs inside 5-6 mega-funds. The 22.5pp top/bottom spread in a single month explains the comp numbers.
items.tweetA: Hedge fund industry at $5T AUM. In April alone: top decile returned +18.4%, bottom decile returned -4.1%. A 22.5 percentage point spread in a SINGLE month. 'Compensation is no longer driven by salary. It's driven by positioning discipline during regime shifts.' The AUM number is not the story.
items.tweetB: Jane Street paid an average of $2.7M per employee in 2025. 700 people. $1.9B total comp. And if you tried to leave and start your own fund? Kurt Baker — a decade at Millennium running Asia — tried a $3B launch. Raised to 50%, never launched. Cold raises are dead. You launch as a satellite of a mega-fund or you don't launch.

charts.desc: No charts in email.

items.title: Google Cloud + AWS both step into x402 agentic payment flow — phase shift in one week
items.idea: The Solana Foundation and Google Cloud released Pay.sh — agent discovery/access toolkit with authorized access to Gemini, BigQuery, Vertex AI. Days later, Coinbase announced x402 integration into Amazon Bedrock AgentCore Payments. Two weeks prior the author flagged x402's authorization problem with unauthorized third-party wrappers. Now the two largest cloud providers are directly in the flow. PayAI, Crossmint, MoonPay, and Sponge complete the stack. Coinbase simultaneously laid off 14% to 'reshape operations around AI.'
items.angle: This is the fastest validation cycle I've tracked in agent-payments. The question now is who owns the rails: Ethereum or Solana. Pay.sh is Solana's move. ERC-8257 and ACTA are Ethereum's response.
items.tweetA: Two weeks ago x402's biggest risk was unauthorized API wrappers turning partners into adversaries. This week Google Cloud and AWS stepped directly into the agent-payment flow. The hyperscalers saw what we see — and moved faster than expected. The rails for AI-to-AI payments are being built right now.
items.tweetB: Google Cloud, AWS, and Coinbase (cutting 14% to 'reshape around AI') are all now in the x402 agentic payment stack. The question isn't whether AI agents will transact on-chain. It's whether Ethereum or Solana own the rails. Pay.sh is Solana's move. ERC-8257 is Ethereum's answer. $SOL $ETH

charts.desc: No charts.

items.title: Meta FAIR Autodata: 34-point accuracy gap — inference compute is now a data quality lever
items.idea: Meta FAIR (Jason Weston et al.) introduced Autodata, an agentic data scientist that generates, critiques, and refines training and evaluation data in a closed loop. On a CS research QA task, Autodata data opened a 34-point accuracy gap between weak and strong models — far larger than off-the-shelf instruction sets achieve. Reframes synthetic data as the primary place where inference budget pays off — not just inference, but training quality.
items.angle: If you can convert inference compute into training data quality, inference scale IS training scale. The labs with the biggest inference budgets win the next round of training — a very different game than raw parameter count.
items.tweetA: Meta FAIR just showed an agentic data scientist that opens a 34-point accuracy gap between weak and strong models — much larger than standard instruction sets. The key reframe: inference compute is a data quality lever, not just an inference lever. The models that win will generate the best training data for themselves.
items.tweetB: Every AI lab is racing to scale inference. Meta FAIR is racing to convert inference compute into training data quality. If Autodata's 34-point gap holds: the lab with the best inference budget wins the next training round. That's a fundamentally different game than parameter scale. $META

charts.desc: No charts in email.

items.title: Claude Managed Agents: Dreaming + Outcomes + multi-agent — Harvey 6x, Wisedocs -50%
items.idea: Anthropic added three capabilities to Claude Managed Agents: (1) 'Dreaming' — agents review prior sessions to update their own memory between runs; (2) 'Outcomes' — grading rubrics that evaluate and reject outputs until standards are met; (3) orchestration layer enabling lead agents to delegate to specialist subagents. Early results: Harvey (legal AI) reports 6x completion gains; Wisedocs cut review time by 50%.
items.angle: 'Dreaming' changes the agent reliability calculus. Agents that review prior work and update their own memory are fundamentally different from stateless agents. Harvey's 6x and Wisedocs's 50% suggest the enterprise adoption case just meaningfully strengthened.
items.tweetA: Anthropic added 'Dreaming' to Claude Managed Agents — agents now review prior sessions to update their own memory state between runs. Combined with self-grading 'Outcomes' and multi-agent orchestration: Harvey reports 6x completion gains, Wisedocs cut review time 50%. Stateless agents were the bottleneck. This removes it.
items.tweetB: The word choice matters: 'Dreaming.' Agents that consolidate experience into updated memory between sessions are no longer tools — they're something closer to practitioners. Harvey 6x. Wisedocs 50% reduction. The enterprise AI adoption story just got a new chapter. $ALAB $SOUN

charts.desc: No charts.

items.title: $700B hyperscaler AI capex (+77% YoY), OpenAI missed revenue targets, Mag 7 now leveraged capex vehicles
items.idea: Combined hyperscaler AI capex for 2026 now tracks ~$700B, up 77% YoY. Meta raised guidance to $125-145B and dropped 8% the next day. WSJ reported OpenAI missed internal revenue targets and its CFO worried privately about paying compute bills. IGV down ~16% YTD, Salesforce -35%, ServiceNow -40%. Ted Oakley: 'Mag 7 balance sheets have shifted from cash machines to leveraged capex vehicles.' Steve Hanke: US productivity actually fell last year. Aleksandra Przegalinska (Harvard Law): 'one engineer using agentic AI now does the work of fifty.' Michael Gayed: 'labor market will split into the architects and the unemployed.'
items.angle: The OpenAI revenue miss is the most consequential piece of information. $700B in capex is being justified by a demand curve that hasn't arrived yet. If OpenAI's CFO is privately worried about paying the compute bill, that's the entire investment thesis at risk.
items.tweetA: $700B in AI capex in 2026. +77% year-over-year. And the primary customer — OpenAI — has a CFO who privately wonders if they can pay the compute bill. Meta raised guidance to $125-145B and dropped 8%. Mag 7 balance sheets are now 'leveraged capex vehicles.' IGV down 16%. Salesforce -35%. ServiceNow -40%.
items.tweetB: Either $700B in AI capex produces economic returns that justify the investment — or we're watching the largest misallocation in corporate history, financed by debt and paid for by laying off white-collar workers. The companies writing those checks (Meta, Microsoft, Google) are the ones firing. The math has to work eventually.

charts.desc: Compilation video references, no charts in email.

items.title: What the Warsh Fed should actually do: shorten SOMA maturity, not hike — labor in 'unstable equilibrium'
items.idea: Ironsides argues pandemic inflation was fiscal/monetary (not just supply chains), and the Warsh Fed's appropriate tool is to reduce the average maturity of its SOMA bond portfolio — not hike rates. Hiking hurts small businesses disproportionately. Current labor market: headline U3 at 4.3% looks stable, but household survey shows -1.3M jobs YTD, labor force contracting, participation at Oct 2021 lows. Correction triggers identified: monetary policy mistake on energy prices, 2Q26 earnings growth scare, or 'monetization shocks' when OpenAI/Anthropic IPO.
items.angle: The 'monetization shock' trigger from OpenAI/Anthropic IPOs is a new idea — the market would have to price in a massive supply of equity at the same time these companies are burning cash. The SOMA maturity shortening argument is more sophisticated than the standard hike/cut binary.
items.tweetA: Ironsides: the Warsh Fed should fight inflation by shortening the maturity of its bond portfolio (SOMA), not by hiking rates. Hiking kills small businesses without fixing supply-side inflation. The tool that fits the problem isn't the tool everyone expects to be used.
items.tweetB: The Fed's headline problem: U3 'stable' at 4.3%. The household survey problem: -1.3M jobs YTD, labor force contracting, participation at its lowest since Oct 2021. 'Unstable equilibrium.' Next correction trigger Ironsides flags: 'monetization shocks' when OpenAI and Anthropic IPO. The float coming to market is enormous.

items.title: USD/CAD as 'VIX of the currency complex' — tech parabolas need a compass, not a ruler
items.idea: Dale Pinkert (50-year trading career) argues USD/CAD has bottomed and is the leading indicator for broader market volatility — the 'VIX of the currency complex.' Despite the de-dollarization narrative, the dollar hasn't made new lows. On tech: 'blow-off action unlike anything in 50-year career' — charts so vertical you can't draw a trend line. 'You need a compass, not a ruler.' VIX shows a 'hammer' candlestick (downside momentum exhausting). Still bearish on gold (watching 4,670), won't turn bullish until close above 5,000. TLT target: 82.
items.angle: The USD/CAD vol-leading signal is the most actionable specific call here. If Pinkert is right and USD/CAD is the currency VIX, a bounce from current levels would be the first warning flag for a broader risk-off event.
items.tweetA: Dale Pinkert (50 years in markets): USD/CAD has bottomed. He calls it the 'VIX of the currency complex' — historically a leading indicator of broader vol spikes. Meanwhile he's looking at Micron and SanDisk charts so vertical 'you can't draw a trend line anymore.' Compass, not ruler.
items.tweetB: 'You would think — with all the de-dollarization, the basement trade — that the dollar would have already made new lows.' It hasn't. The consensus dollar collapse hasn't landed. Pinkert is buying USD/CAD pullbacks. If he's right on vol, the implications cascade across every carry and commodity trade.

items.title: Natural gas BOIL: 'buying what everyone hates' — Super El Nino + strategic reserves running out
items.idea: Pinkert's ultimate contrarian pick: natural gas via BOIL ETF. Move above 2.75 = trend change signal. The 'largest release of strategic reserves in history' has cushioned crude prices but 'is going to run out.' A super El Nino pattern is building in the Pacific — watching wheat at 5.40 for one more leg down before summer solstice. If tech corrects, Pinkert expects rotation to ag/fertilizer/energy. Bonds: still targeting 82 on TLT, bond bulls 'whistling past the graveyard.'
items.angle: Strategic reserves exhaustion timing combined with Memorial Day gas-price political signal creates a specific catalyst window. Nat gas is also the cleanest way to play data-center power demand without oil war risk.
items.tweetA: Pinkert's contrarian pick: natural gas (BOIL). 'Buying what everyone hates.' Move above 2.75 = trend change. He also sees a super El Nino forming in the Pacific — watching wheat at 5.40 for one more leg down before summer solstice. If tech rolls over, he rotates to ag, fertilizer, energy. $BOIL $WEAT
items.tweetB: The 'largest release of strategic reserves in history' is what's been cushioning crude. Pinkert: 'It's going to run out.' December crude already below $70 while spot is $101. If the ceasefire talks collapse AND reserves run out simultaneously — that's a very different oil price by Q3. $USO $XLE

items.title: Soloway sold 99% of longs, rotated to shorts — Intel P/E 80-90, Nasdaq/M2 exceeds dot-com peak
items.idea: Gareth Soloway bought aggressively at the March 30 Iran-selloff lows using contrarian signals (oil blowoff top, retail calling for $200 oil). Has since sold 99% of those longs and rotated to shorts. Intel at $100/share, $500B market cap, forward P/E 80-90: 'irrational exuberance of late 1999.' Nasdaq overlaid against M2 money supply: ratio now exceeds the dot-com peak. Michael Burry publicly comparing Philly Semi (+65% YTD) action to late 1999. Nasdaq rallied 22% off March 30 lows in a single month.
items.angle: 99% of longs sold → shorts initiated is the most concrete positioning statement in this window. Combined with Burry's 1999 comparison on Philly Semi and the Macro Mornings concentration data, the bearish case is converging from multiple independent sources.
items.tweetA: Gareth Soloway bought aggressively at the March 30 Iran selloff (oil blowoff top, CNBC calling for $200 oil were his contrarian signals). He's since sold 99% of those longs and rotated to shorts. Intel at $100/share, P/E 80-90. Michael Burry comparing Philly Semi (+65% YTD) to late 1999. The warning system is going off.
items.tweetB: The Nasdaq/M2 ratio now exceeds the dot-com peak. Soloway showed the overlay live. If M2 growth explains asset prices, the Nasdaq is MORE overvalued relative to liquidity than it was in 2000. That is the chart nobody wants to look at right now. $QQQ $INTC $MU

items.title: 'Stablecoin' is fintech's 'horsepower' — the name won't survive, but the technology will
items.idea: Robert Hackett (a16z) argues 'stablecoin' is a name born to solve a problem (crypto volatility) that no longer defines the category. Stability is now table stakes — a prerequisite, not the point. Stablecoins have become 'foundational infrastructure for a new global financial system': instant cross-border value transfer, immediate settlement, programmable, no intermediary. The question shifted from 'will it hold its value?' to 'what can we build with it?' The term will likely fade as the technology becomes default.
items.angle: The naming shift has real regulatory implications: regulations built around 'stablecoin' will need to be rebuilt around 'digital dollar / programmable money.' The naming lag creates regulatory arbitrage windows.
items.tweetA: 'Stablecoin' is the 'horsepower' of fintech. The name was born when volatility was the problem. Now stability is table stakes. The product is programmable money — instant settlement, no intermediary, embeddable in any application. The question shifted from 'will it hold?' to 'what can we build?' The name hasn't kept up with the technology.
items.tweetB: We still 'dial' numbers. We still 'film' things. We 'cc' people. a16z's Robert Hackett: stablecoins will follow the same path as 'electric lighting' — the term disappears when the technology becomes the default. This isn't a rebrand essay. It's a maturation thesis. And it has real regulatory implications. $COIN $USDC

items.title: Semis only sector at ATH — banks flat, Citi Euphoria spiking, insiders sitting on their hands
items.idea: Helene Meisler's Saturday Chart Fest: Semis (SOX) are the ONLY group that made new highs last week. Banks flat on the year. Transports 'should have rallied more with oil coming down' but haven't. Utilities: lower low. IWM lagging S&P and Nasdaq. The Citi Panic/Euphoria model is 'Getting Euphoric again.' Corporate insiders: 'sitting on their hands.' McClellan Summation Index: 'April has been a dud. No rollover. Yet.' XLB (materials): 'was that a false breakout?'
items.angle: The insiders-sitting-on-hands data is most actionable. When the people with best information on company fundamentals aren't buying their own stocks at ATH, that's a signal. Combined with Citi Euphoria turning, the setup is increasingly one-sided.
items.tweetA: Helene Meisler's Saturday charts: Semis (SOX) are the ONLY sector making new highs. Banks flat on the year. Transports underperforming despite oil falling. Citi Panic/Euphoria: 'Getting Euphoric again.' Insiders: 'sitting on their hands.' The breadth picture is as narrow as the concentration data from Macro Mornings.
items.tweetB: 'Getting Euphoric again' — Citi Panic/Euphoria model. Insiders sitting on their hands. Banks flat YTD while the Philly Semi is +65%. Last time this combination showed up — euphoria + insider inaction — you know how the pattern resolved. McClellan Summation: April dud. No rollover. Yet.

charts.desc: 25+ charts across sectors: DJIA, Transports, SPX, Naz, IWM, QQQ, SOX, Banks, IGV, XLI, XLE, XME, XLV, GLD, URA, VGK, KWEB, DXY, 10yr yield, McClellan, Citi P/E, Insiders. CHART WORTH SAVING: Citi Panic/Euphoria showing euphoria re-emerging + Insider chart showing inaction.

items.title: Cerebras $26.6B IPO, SpaceX $1.75T June offering, Terafab $55B+ Phase 1, Samsung $1T on HBM
items.idea: Four hardware/infrastructure milestones: (1) Cerebras targeting $26.6B IPO valuation, $3.5B raise, $510M 2025 revenue, multi-year OpenAI compute agreement >$20B; (2) SpaceX reportedly targeting June 2026 IPO at $1.75T valuation — would be the largest public offering in history; OpenAI + Anthropic at ~$2T combined still private; (3) Elon's Terafab: first phase may cost $55B+ using Intel 14A; (4) Samsung hit $1T market cap on its largest single-day gain, driven by HBM demand and Apple US chip production discussions.
items.angle: SpaceX at $1.75T would be larger than any prior public offering. If it prices in June, it creates the most significant index inclusion event in history and a massive absorber of retail/institutional capital — directly competing with existing tech positions for flow.
items.tweetA: Three hardware milestones in one week: Samsung hits $1T on HBM demand (largest single-day gain ever). Cerebras targeting $26.6B IPO ($510M revenue, $20B+ OpenAI compute deal). Elon's Terafab: Phase 1 alone may cost $55B+ using Intel 14A. The AI buildout is being capitalized at a scale that has no precedent.
items.tweetB: SpaceX reportedly targeting a June 2026 IPO at $1.75T — larger than any prior public offering in history. OpenAI + Anthropic at ~$2T combined, still private. The most valuable companies on earth are staying off public markets. When SpaceX does list, the capital absorption will be unlike anything index funds have ever had to absorb.

charts.desc: No charts. News aggregation.

items.title: China controls 99% of gallium — 24 export restrictions, US munitions at 81% THAAD depletion
items.idea: Nadrowski (Amvest Terraden): China controls ~99% of gallium, 82% tungsten, comparable shares of heavy rare earths, ~50% bismuth/vanadium/antimony/indium. 24 separate export control restrictions through Oct 2025. Gallium/germanium restrictions: 'suspended 12 months, not lifted — crisis is intact.' US munitions depletion in Iran conflict: 27% Tomahawk, 23% JASSM, 78% PrSM, 61% SM-3, 32% SM-6, 81% THAAD (3 radars destroyed), 61% Patriot. CSIS: 4-6 years to rebuild. US vs. China shipbuilding: 232:1 annually.
items.angle: 81% THAAD depletion with 3 radars destroyed is the most alarming single figure. THAAD defends against medium/long-range ballistic missiles — the precise category Iran and North Korea rely on. Cannot be rebuilt quickly. Most consequential defense procurement gap of the decade.
items.tweetA: China controls 99% of gallium. 82% of tungsten. ~50% of antimony, vanadium, bismuth. And US munitions after the Iran conflict: 81% of THAAD gone (3 radars destroyed), 61% of Patriots, 78% of PrSMs expended. CSIS: 4-6 years to rebuild. The critical minerals crisis and the munitions crisis are the same crisis.
items.tweetB: Gallium and germanium export restrictions: 'suspended for 12 months, not lifted.' The crisis is intact. Western gallium production is at roughly 1% of non-Chinese supply. In 1973 the OPEC embargo produced the petrodollar arrangement. What's the 2026 equivalent for critical minerals? Nobody has built it.

items.title: Prompt injection on Grok Bankr: $150k drained — AI agent wallets are the new attack surface
items.idea: Someone prompt-injected Grok's Bankr AI wallet and drained ~$150k worth of DRB tokens (80% later returned). As AI agents increasingly handle real capital through x402 and agentic payment stacks, prompt injection is the primary attack vector. Two protocol-level responses: ERC-8257 (simple onchain gating of x402 payments) and ACTA (anonymous credentials for trustless agents — privacy layer atop ERC-8004 for agent identity/reputation proofs without exposing underlying data).
items.angle: The prompt injection attack on an AI wallet is the category-defining security event for agentic finance. This will happen repeatedly and at larger scale as agent wallets grow. ACTA and ERC-8257 are the infrastructure answers.
items.tweetA: The first serious prompt injection on an AI crypto wallet happened this week. Someone drained ~$150k from Grok's Bankr agent. 80% returned — but the attack was real. As AI agents handle more capital through x402 and agentic payments, this is the category of attack that will scale. ERC-8257 and ACTA are the infrastructure response.
items.tweetB: Prompt injection just graduated from 'lab demonstration' to 'live theft of $150k from an AI wallet.' This is the canonical security failure mode for agentic finance. The question is whether ACTA (agent identity) and ERC-8257 (payment gating) can ship before the attack surface becomes systemic. $SOL $ETH

charts.desc: No charts.

items.title: Social media usage declining across ALL age brackets simultaneously — first time ever
items.idea: Daily social media usage has declined across every major age bracket simultaneously — described as happening for the first time after years of uninterrupted growth. The sharpest drop is among younger users. The question: are chatbots, private groups, or fatigue replacing public feeds? The attention economy's core assumption — indefinite growth in scrolling time — may have peaked without a formal announcement.
items.angle: If true, this is the most important demographic signal for digital advertising in a decade. You can't behavioral-target a conversation with Claude. The TAM for attention-based advertising may have just peaked. This is the thesis that shorts Meta at scale over the next 5 years.
items.tweetA: Social media daily usage just declined across ALL age brackets simultaneously. For the first time. The sharpest drop: younger users. The theory: chatbots, private groups, and fatigue are replacing feeds. If you can't behavioral-target a Claude conversation or a Telegram group — the ad-targeting TAM may have peaked. $META $SNAP
items.tweetB: We obsess over AI replacing jobs. The quietly more important shift: AI replacing attention. If younger users are spending time with chatbots instead of social feeds, the behavioral data that powers ad targeting disappears. You can't monetize a private conversation. The attention economy's growth assumption just hit a wall.

charts.desc: No charts. Stat referenced without primary source — worth tracking down.

items.title: ESAB management calls itself a 'compounder' — Greenberg: real compounders never have to say so
items.idea: After Herb Greenberg red-flagged ESAB ($ESAB) as a 'false narrative' compounder, Q1 earnings call showed a sharp increase in the word 'compounder.' Greenberg's rule: 'Companies that are compounders don't have to tell you they are.' On UPS ($UPS): after 16 consecutive years of dividend raises, new CFO Brian Dykes subtly changed language around the dividend in March. On Vital Farms ($VITL): after a 65% spike post-red-flag, Q1 showed earnings miss, guidance cut, retreat from expansion to focus on eggs. Analysts calling CoreWeave earnings a 'great quarter' and management thanking them for their 'partnership.'
items.angle: The 'partnership' language between sell-side analysts and CoreWeave is the red-flag. That's the kind of detail that surfaces in SEC enforcement actions. ESAB's adoption of its critics' language is the general signal: when companies adopt the language of their critics, the jig is usually up.
items.tweetA: 'Companies that are compounders don't have to tell you they are.' — Herb Greenberg. ESAB $ESAB peppered its Q1 call with 'compounder' after he red-flagged the narrative. UPS $UPS new CFO changed language on the 16-consecutive-year dividend. Vital Farms $VITL: 65% spike, now guidance cut, retreating to 'just eggs.' These are signals.
items.tweetB: Buried in Herb Greenberg's Wrap: an analyst gave 'big congrats' on CoreWeave's $CRWV earnings call. Management thanked them for their 'partnership since our IPO.' Partnership. Between an analyst and the company they cover. 'As always, interpret at will.' I've interpreted.

charts.desc: ESAB word-count chart showing spike in 'compounder' usage on earnings call.

items.title: Gold $4,715, WGC record Q1 demand 1,230.9 tonnes — Bank of Canada holds ZERO gold (only G7 member)
items.idea: WGC reported record first-quarter global gold demand of 1,230.9 metric tonnes. Gold rose ~3% for the week to ~$4,715/oz. Silver near $76/oz. Multiple analyst targets: $6,000/oz before year-end (Horwitz, Wells Fargo, European banks). BRICS central bank reserves: rising from 11% to 17% in gold. The Bank of Canada holds ZERO gold — the only G7 central bank in that position. Former Yukon Premier Ranj Pillai argues Canada is watching BRICS accumulation more carefully than its public posture suggests.
items.angle: Bank of Canada holding zero gold as BRICS moves from 11% to 17% of reserves in gold is the most structurally interesting geopolitical/monetary fact in this batch. If Canada is forced to rebalance, even to 5%, the marginal buyer pressure on gold is enormous.
items.tweetA: WGC: record Q1 gold demand — 1,230.9 metric tonnes globally. BRICS central bank reserves rising from 11% to 17% in gold. The Bank of Canada holds ZERO gold — the only G7 central bank in that position. European banks and Wells Fargo flagging $6,000/oz as attainable. Gold at $4,715 is not the story. The buyer composition is.
items.tweetB: Gold demand hit a record in Q1 2026. BRICS moving from 11% to 17% of reserves in gold. The Bank of Canada is the only G7 central bank with zero gold. If Canada eventually rebalances to even 5% — that's a marginal buyer at a scale the market hasn't priced. The de-dollarization trade has a gold lever most people aren't watching.

items.title: DJI banned, 80% of US police drones were Chinese — TAV building modular replacement, Chinese edge is 4-day lead times
items.idea: The December 2025 FCC ban on new DJI imports created a market gap: DJI had 70-80% global consumer drone market share and 80% of US police drones. American alternatives are typically 5x the price. TAV Drones (Tom Rein, UT Austin freshman) is building modular police drones — one airframe swaps between zoom, thermal cameras, motors for multiple use cases. China's manufacturing edge: standardized component ecosystems compress lead times to 4 days. TAV's response: consolidate 10 parts into 1 complex part + patented design. Iranian Shaheds cost $30-35k vs. US interceptors costing several multiples more per shot.
items.angle: The DJI ban is a government-mandated market creation event. Police/public safety drones are a large, immediate TAM that is price-sensitive in a way that makes domestic supply viable. Counter-drone tech is the adjacent investment thesis.
items.tweetA: The FCC banned DJI imports in December 2025. DJI had 70-80% global consumer share and 80% of US police drones. American alternatives are 5x the price. A freshman at UT Austin is building modular police drones to fill the gap. China's edge isn't cheap labor — it's 4-day lead times from standardized component ecosystems.
items.tweetB: Iranian Shaheds: $30-35k each. US interceptor missiles: several multiples more per shot. 'Gatling-style point defense proved less effective against drone swarms than advertised.' Counter-UAS funding is the fastest-growing defense-adjacent VC category right now. The asymmetry is too obvious to ignore much longer. $LHX $AXON
